title: Flatfish
content:
A flatfish is a member of the ray-finned demersal fish order Pleuronectiformes, also called the Heterosomata, sometimes classified as a suborder of Perciformes. In many species, both eyes lie on one side of the head, one or the other migrating through or around the head during development. Some species face their left sides upward, some face their right sides upward, and others face either side upward. Many important food fish are in this order, including the flounder s, sole s, turbot, plaice, and halibut. Some flatfish can camouflage themselves on the ocean floor. Taxonomy. Over 800 described species are placed into 16 families.

Broadly, the flatfishes are divided into two suborders, Psettodoidei and Pleuronectoidei, with 99% of the species diversity found within the Pleuronectoidei. The largest families are <a href="Soleidae" Soleidae</a, <a href="Bothidae" Bothidae</a and <a href="Tonguefish" Cynoglossidae</a with more than 150 species each. There also exist two monotypic families (<a href="Paralichthodidae" Paralichthodidae</a and <a href="Oncopteridae" Oncopteridae</a). Some families are the results of relatively recent splits.

For example, the <a href="Achiridae" Achiridae</a were classified as a subfamily of Soleidae in the past, and the <a href="Samaridae" Samaridae</a were considered a subfamily of the Pleuronectidae. The families <a href="Paralichthodidae" Paralichthodidae</a, <a href="Poecilopsettidae" Poecilopsettidae</a, and <a href="Rhombosoleidae" Rhombosoleidae</a were also traditionally treated as subfamilies of Pleuronectidae, but are now recognised as families in their own right.

The <a href="Paralichthyidae" Paralichthyidae</a has long been indicated to be paraphyletic, with the formal description of <a href="Cyclopsettidae" Cyclopsettidae</a in 2019 resulting in the split of this family as well. The <a href="Taxonomy%20%28biology%29" taxonomy</a of some groups is in need of a review, as the last <a href="monograph" monograph</a covering the entire order was <a href="John%20Roxborough%20Norman" John Roxborough Norman</a's "Monograph of the Flatfishes" published in 1934.

In particular, "<a href="Tephrinectes%20sinensis"Tephrinectes sinensis</a" may represent a family-level lineage and requires further evaluation e.g. <a href="New%20species" New species</a are described with some regularity and <a href="undescribed%20species" undescribed species</a likely remain. Hybrids. <a href="Hybrid%20%28biology%29" Hybrids</a are well known in flatfishes. The Pleuronectidae, of marine fishes, have the largest number of reported hybrids.
